content.text: are an extension of an AWS Region that place select compute, storage, and networking services inside a specific metro area. The goal is single-digit to low-double-digit millisecond latency to users in that city — latency that a distant Region cannot match because of the speed of light across hundreds of miles of fiber. A Local Zone is not an independent Region: it shares the parent Region's VPC, IAM, CloudWatch, and control plane. Only the compute layer runs locally.

attrs.alt: AWS Local Zone architecture: local compute in LA metro connected via AWS backbone to us-west-2 parent Region, sharing VPC and control plane

content.text: AWS Regions are placed where large concentrations of enterprise customers exist and where power, land, and network infrastructure make full three-AZ deployments cost-effective. Regions are not placed in every major city. Local Zones fill the gap: they bring EC2, EBS, ELB, and a handful of other services to cities that have latency-sensitive workloads but not enough demand to justify a full Region.
The rule of thumb: if your application needs
to users in a specific city, and that city is not close to a Region, a Local Zone is the solution.
: video production pipelines, real-time rendering, and streaming platforms co-located with Hollywood studios (LA Local Zone).
: low-latency game servers for competitive players in dense metro markets.
: high-frequency trading and tick data processing requiring sub-5ms latency to specific exchange data centers.
: real-time AR/VR sessions where > 20 ms breaks immersion.
Local Zones use a naming convention that makes their location explicit:
. The availability zone ID inside a Local Zone follows the same format.

# List all Local Zones available in the current account/region
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --filters Name=zone-type,Values=local-zone \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].{ZoneName:ZoneName,ZoneId:ZoneId,State:State,ParentZoneName:ParentZoneName}' \
  --output table

# List only opted-in Local Zones in us-east-1
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --filters Name=zone-type,Values=local-zone \
           Name=state,Values=available \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].ZoneName'
Opting In to a Local Zone

Local Zones are disabled by default in every AWS account. You must explicitly opt in to each Local Zone you want to use. The opt-in is account-level and region-level — it does not require any policy change.

# Opt in to a specific Local Zone
aws ec2 modify-availability-zone-group \
  --group-name us-west-2-lax-1 \
  --opt-in-status opted-in \
  --region us-west-2

# Verify opt-in status
aws ec2 describe-availability-zone-groups \
  --filters Name=group-name,Values=us-west-2-lax-1 \
  --query 'AvailabilityZoneGroups[*].{Name:GroupName,Status:OptInStatus}'

# Opt in via console: EC2 → Account Attributes → Zones → Manage
# The opt-in propagates within seconds; no restart required
Networking: VPC Subnets in a Local Zone

A Local Zone extends your existing VPC by adding subnets in the Local Zone's availability zone. Instances in the Local Zone subnet communicate with instances in the parent Region's subnets over the AWS backbone — the same VPC, the same route tables, the same security groups.

# Get the AZ name for the LA Local Zone
LZ_AZ=$(aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --filters Name=zone-name,Values=us-west-2-lax-1a \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[0].ZoneName' \
  --output text --region us-west-2)

# Create a subnet in the Local Zone (using an existing VPC)
aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc123def456789 \
  --cidr-block 10.0.128.0/24 \
  --availability-zone $LZ_AZ \
  --region us-west-2 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=subnet,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=lax-subnet}]'

# Associate a route table (same as or separate from the Region subnets)
aws ec2 associate-route-table \
  --subnet-id subnet-0lax123 \
  --route-table-id rtb-0abc123 \
  --region us-west-2

Important: a Local Zone subnet and a Region AZ subnet are in the same VPC. Security groups and NACLs apply across both. Traffic between them travels over the AWS private backbone — it does not go through the public internet.

Local Zones offer a subset of what a Region provides. The core compute and storage stack is available; most managed database services, serverless compute (Lambda), and storage-class services (S3, DynamoDB) are not available locally — they are accessed via the backbone from the parent Region.
ECS (EC2 launch type), EKS (worker nodes only)
ECS Fargate, ECR (images pulled from Region)
Launching EC2 in a Local Zone

# Launch an EC2 instance in the LA Local Zone
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --instance-type c5.xlarge \
  --subnet-id subnet-0lax123 \
  --security-group-ids sg-0abc123 \
  --key-name my-key-pair \
  --placement AvailabilityZone=us-west-2-lax-1a \
  --block-device-mappings '[{
    "DeviceName": "/dev/xvda",
    "Ebs": {"VolumeSize": 30, "VolumeType": "gp3"}
  }]' \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=lax-app-server}]' \
  --region us-west-2

# Verify the instance is in the Local Zone
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters Name=tag:Name,Values=lax-app-server \
  --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].{AZ:Placement.AvailabilityZone,State:State.Name}'
Load Balancing in a Local Zone

You can place an Application Load Balancer (ALB) or Network Load Balancer (NLB) directly in a Local Zone subnet. The LB distributes traffic to EC2 targets in the same Local Zone, keeping all traffic local to the metro area.

# Create an ALB in the LA Local Zone subnet
aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
  --name lax-alb \
  --type application \
  --scheme internet-facing \
  --subnets subnet-0lax123 \
  --security-groups sg-0abc123 \
  --region us-west-2

# Create a target group for EC2 instances in the LZ
aws elbv2 create-target-group \
  --name lax-targets \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 8080 \
  --vpc-id vpc-0abc123def456789 \
  --target-type instance \
  --region us-west-2

# Register EC2 instances (must be in the LZ subnet)
aws elbv2 register-targets \
  --target-group-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-west-2:123456789012:targetgroup/lax-targets/abc123 \
  --targets Id=i-0abc123lax Id=i-0def456lax
Local Zones vs Other Specialized Infrastructure
Customer's own data center or colo
EC2, EBS, ELB, RDS, ElastiCache, ECS
EC2, EBS, ECS, EKS — very limited
AWS ships and manages hardware on-site
Terraform: Local Zone Subnet and EC2
Instance Type Availability in Local Zones
Not every EC2 instance type is available in every Local Zone. Before launching, check which types are offered in the specific Local Zone to avoid a capacity error at launch time.

# List instance types available in the LA Local Zone
aws ec2 describe-instance-type-offerings \
  --location-type availability-zone \
  --filters Name=location,Values=us-west-2-lax-1a \
  --query 'InstanceTypeOfferings[*].InstanceType' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | sort

# Typical result includes:
# c5.large  c5.xlarge  c5.2xlarge
# g4dn.xlarge  g4dn.2xlarge  (GPU for rendering)
# m5.large  m5.xlarge
# r5.large  r5.xlarge  (memory-optimized)
# t3.medium  t3.large  (burstable)

# Metal instances and some compute-intensive families may not be available in all LZs
No multi-AZ within a Local Zone

: Each Local Zone is a single AZ. There is no second AZ in the same metro to run a multi-AZ RDS or a multi-AZ ALB within the Local Zone. For HA, run a multi-AZ deployment that spans the LZ and the parent Region.

: Objects stored in the parent Region S3 bucket are accessed over the backbone from the Local Zone. For write-intensive workloads, this ~10 ms overhead matters — use EBS for latency-sensitive local storage and S3 for bulk/archival.
: Local Zones have smaller hardware footprints than Regions. On-demand capacity may be constrained during peaks; use
in the Local Zone AZ to guarantee capacity for critical instances.
: Not all AMIs are available in all Local Zones. Use AWS-provided AMIs (Amazon Linux 2023, Ubuntu) which are replicated to Local Zones, or copy your custom AMI to the Local Zone before launch.

: EC2 in Local Zones typically costs 10–20% more than equivalent instances in the parent Region; EBS pricing is also slightly higher. Factor this in when deciding whether a LZ is justified over a Region-based deployment.

# Reserve capacity in the LA Local Zone AZ
aws ec2 create-capacity-reservation \
  --instance-type c5.xlarge \
  --instance-platform Linux/UNIX \
  --availability-zone us-west-2-lax-1a \
  --instance-count 4 \
  --instance-match-criteria targeted \
  --region us-west-2

# Copy an AMI from the Region to the Local Zone
# (AMIs are AZ-agnostic within a Region — no explicit copy needed;
#  if the AMI isn't available, register a new one from the snapshot)
aws ec2 copy-image \
  --source-image-id ami-0source12345 \
  --source-region us-west-2 \
  --region us-west-2 \
  --name 'my-app-image-for-lax'
CloudWatch agents on Local Zone instances forward metrics to the parent Region's CloudWatch endpoint. There is no separate CloudWatch endpoint in the Local Zone — metrics travel over the backbone.

# Install and configure CloudWatch agent on a Local Zone EC2 instance
# (same process as Region-based instances)
sudo yum install -y amazon-cloudwatch-agent

# Create a config for CPU, memory, and disk metrics
sudo /opt/aws/amazon-cloudwatch-agent/bin/amazon-cloudwatch-agent-config-wizard

# Start the agent
sudo systemctl enable amazon-cloudwatch-agent
sudo systemctl start amazon-cloudwatch-agent

# Metrics appear in CloudWatch under:
# Namespace: CWAgent
# Dimension: InstanceId=i-0lax..., ImageId, InstanceType
# Note: the AvailabilityZone dimension will show 'us-west-2-lax-1a' —
# use this to filter dashboards to Local Zone instances specifically

# Create a CloudWatch alarm on LZ instances
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name lax-high-cpu \
  --namespace CWAgent \
  --metric-name cpu_usage_active \
  --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=i-0lax123 Name=AvailabilityZone,Value=us-west-2-lax-1a \
  --period 60 --evaluation-periods 3 \
  --threshold 80 --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --statistic Average \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-west-2:123456789012:ops-alerts
High Availability Pattern: Local Zone + Parent Region

A Local Zone is a single AZ — it has no built-in redundancy. To design for fault tolerance, run your application in both the Local Zone and at least one AZ in the parent Region. Route 53 latency routing or an ALB with cross-zone load balancing can distribute traffic and fail over when the Local Zone is unavailable.

# Multi-target ALB spanning Local Zone and parent Region AZ
# Both subnets are in the same VPC; ALB needs at least 2 subnets in different AZs
# Note: ALB across LZ + Region AZ is supported; cross-zone LB is recommended

aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
  --name lax-multi-az-alb \
  --type application \
  --scheme internet-facing \
  --subnets subnet-0lax1a subnet-0usw2a \
  --security-groups sg-0abc123 \
  --region us-west-2

# Register targets from both Local Zone and Region AZ in the same target group
aws elbv2 register-targets \
  --target-group-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-west-2:123456789012:targetgroup/multi-az/abc \
  --targets Id=i-0lax123 Id=i-0region456

# Enable cross-zone load balancing so ALB distributes evenly
aws elbv2 modify-load-balancer-attributes \
  --load-balancer-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-west-2:123456789012:loadbalancer/app/lax-multi-az-alb/abc \
  --attributes Key=load_balancing.cross_zone.enabled,Value=true
Impact on LZ + Region HA Deployment
Route 53/ALB shifts traffic to Region AZ; ~5–10 ms extra latency
Automatic failover to Region within Route 53 health check interval
LZ continues serving; Region redundancy handles Region-side failures
LZ loses control plane; instances keep running but not manageable
Use multi-region architecture for this risk tier
Using EKS Worker Nodes in a Local Zone

Amazon EKS control planes always run in the parent Region. You can extend an EKS cluster to the Local Zone by creating a node group using a Local Zone subnet. Pods scheduled on these nodes run locally and serve metro users with low latency; the cluster API server communicates with nodes via the backbone.

# Add a Local Zone node group to an existing EKS cluster
aws eks create-nodegroup \
  --cluster-name my-cluster \
  --nodegroup-name lax-workers \
  --subnets subnet-0lax1a \
  --node-role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/EKSNodeRole \
  --ami-type AL2_x86_64 \
  --instance-types c5.xlarge \
  --scaling-config minSize=1,maxSize=5,desiredSize=2 \
  --region us-west-2

# Label nodes by Local Zone to schedule latency-sensitive pods to them
kubectl label nodes -l topology.kubernetes.io/zone=us-west-2-lax-1a \
  local-zone=lax

# Pod spec: schedule to LZ nodes via nodeSelector
cat << 'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    topology.kubernetes.io/zone: us-west-2-lax-1a
  containers:
    - name: low-latency-app
      image: myapp:latest
EOF

as a dedicated private connectivity option. A Direct Connect connection to a Local Zone brings your on-premises network to the Local Zone directly, without routing through the parent Region. This is particularly valuable for media workflows where large file transfers between on-premises storage and Local Zone EC2 instances would be slow over the backbone.
Not all Local Zones have a co-located Direct Connect location — check the AWS Direct Connect locations list for each LZ specifically.
Direct Connect to a Local Zone uses a separate
— it is not the same physical cross-connect as a Region DX connection.

If a Local Zone does not offer a Direct Connect location, connect to the parent Region and route over the backbone to the LZ subnet — the traffic still travels over AWS infrastructure, not the public internet.
(1G or 10G) to the Local Zone can achieve < 1 ms on-premises-to-LZ latency in the same building or campus.
Cost Estimation for Local Zone Deployments
Local Zone pricing is a premium over the equivalent parent Region. Understand the three main cost components before committing.
10–20% higher per hour than same type in parent Region
Use Savings Plans or Reserved Instances in LZ; they are available
~10% higher than Region; same gp3/io1 types
Use CloudFront in front of LZ for cached content
Route S3 calls through VPC endpoint to avoid NAT GW costs
Standard NAT GW pricing — per hour + per GB
Use a NAT instance in the LZ if cost-sensitive (manual but cheaper)

# Estimate monthly cost of 2x c5.xlarge in LA Local Zone (on-demand)
# us-west-2-lax-1 on-demand c5.xlarge ~ $0.212/hr (check current pricing)

python3 << 'EOF'
hours_per_month = 730
rate_per_hour = 0.212  # c5.xlarge in us-west-2-lax-1 (approx)
count = 2
monthly = hours_per_month * rate_per_hour * count

region_rate = 0.192  # c5.xlarge in us-west-2 (parent Region, approx)
region_monthly = hours_per_month * region_rate * count

print(f'LZ monthly cost: ${monthly:.2f}')
print(f'Region monthly cost: ${region_monthly:.2f}')
print(f'LZ premium: ${monthly - region_monthly:.2f}/month ({(monthly/region_monthly - 1)*100:.1f}%)')
EOF
— they are not independent. VPC, IAM, CloudWatch, and S3 all live in the parent Region; only EC2, EBS, ELB, ElastiCache, and RDS run locally.
Opt-in is required per account per Local Zone — disabled by default to prevent accidental usage and unexpected costs.
) tells you exactly where compute runs and which Region it belongs to.
No multi-AZ within the Local Zone
— for high availability, pair the Local Zone subnet with Region AZ subnets in the same VPC and spread ALB across both.
Check instance type availability before planning capacity — not all families are offered in every Local Zone.
workloads: media rendering, live streaming, competitive gaming servers, and financial tick data processing near specific exchanges.
